rulings.Case Title: RUSSELL UTTERBACK VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative, For Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, October 8, 2024, in Department 301, Line 1. 2. Defendant Honeywell International Inc's., Motion for Summary Judgment, or in the alternative, Summary Adjudication is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence that Plaintiff was exposed to asbestos-containing products attributable to Defendant. (See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) The relevant issue upon consideration of Defendant's Motion is whether Defendant has produced evidence proving that "plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence" to establish that Defendant's products exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co., supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 854.) Having found that Defendant failed to meet its burden, the Court need not rule on Defendant's objections to Plaintiff's opposing evidence under C.C.P. section 437c(q). Defendant failed to address all material allegations in Plaintiff's Complaint. Specifically, Plaintiff alleges that Defendant's asbestos exposure stemmed from two sources: asbestos insulation and asbestos-containing soundproofing materials. (Defense Exhibit F at 2:8-9:23.) However, Defendant's motion, as well at its separate statement, only addresses the alleged exposure from soundproofing materials. A motion for summary judgment must dispose of the entire action or a cause of action. (Code. Civ. Pro Section 437(c).) Defendant's motion fails to address all alleged sources of exposure. As such, Defendant has not met its burden to show that no triable issue of material fact exists. With respect Defendant's Motion for Summary Adjudication, Defendant's UMF No. 45, failed to cite the page and line number to the evidence supporting its arguments. (Cal. Rules of Ct., rule 3.1350(d)(3.) This matter notwithstanding, Defendant has not sustained its initial burden. Defendant's motion fails to address any of the facts, witnesses, or documents disclosed in Plaintiff's discovery responses. (Defendant's Exhibits D, F, H.) Moreover, Defendant's argument that Plaintiff should not recover punitive damages because Plaintiff testified that he had no information that Defendant intended to cause him harm is not persuasive. (UMF Nos. 46 and 47.) A plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting his claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.) (Emphasis added.) The Court cannot infer based on Plaintiff's deposition testimony alone that Plaintiff has no case against Defendant. Thus, Defendant failed to shift its burden. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)

rulings.Case Title: RUSSELL UTTERBACK VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, October 8, 2024, in Department 301, Line 1. 1. Defendant Westburne Supply, Inc's., Motion for Summary Judgment is DENIED. Opposition filed by Plaintiff. Reply filed by Defendant. The Court considered Exhibits A through G of the Wood Declaration. The Court considered Exhibits A through C of the Rose Declaration. Defendant's objections to Plaintiff's deposition testimony and declaration attached to the Opposition for lack of foundation, speculation, assumes facts, contradictory hearsay, misleading, inadmissible contradiction of deposition testimony are OVERRULED. Defendant fails to sustain its initial burden of showing that Plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence that Plaintiff was exposed to asbestos-containing products attributable to Defendant. (See Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826, 855.) The relevant issue upon consideration of Defendant's Motion is whether Defendant has produced evidence proving that "plaintiff does not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, needed evidence" to establish that Defendant's products exposed Plaintiff to asbestos. (Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co., supra, 25 Cal.4th at p. 854.) Defendant's argument that Plaintiff's testimony precludes the possibility of Defendant having been a source of asbestos exposure because Plaintiff did not recall the brand name or manufacture of the pipe at the Chico State Job Site and did not see any writings on or markings on the pipe is not persuasive. Plaintiff testified that he saw P.E. O'Hair trucks deliver pipes to Chico State. (P's Exh. A [P's Depo.] at 93:23; 94-17.) Plaintiff identified the trucks by their bunny logo, and by the writing on the trucks that said P.E. O'Hair. (Id. at. 94:11-14: P's Exh. B [P's Depo.] at 190:2-24.) The fact that Plaintiff did not recall the brand and/or manufacture of the pipes, does not create an inference of non-exposure or of the inability to prove exposure though other sources of evidence than Plaintiff's testimony. A plaintiff's ignorance is by itself insufficient to demonstrate that plaintiff lacks the ability to produce evidence supporting his claim and does not serve to shift defendant's initial burden. (Weber v. John Crane, Inc. (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439.) (Emphasis added.) Additionally, Defendant argues that Plaintiff's only encounter with Transite pipes, or anything supplied by P.E. O'Hair generally, would be in passing when he walked past others working. However, Plaintiff testified that he was present near the pipes, and, at times right next to them, inhaling dust when the pipes were cut or installed. (Id. at 95:2-4; 95:10-12: 95:20-23; 96:2-13.) Defendant fails to foreclose the possibility that Plaintiff may yet prove asbestos exposure and causation attributed to Defendant. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)

rulings.Case Title: RUSSELL UTTERBACK VS. CARRIER CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEFENDANT WESTBURNE SUPPLY, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT (ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR TENTATIVE RULING ENTRY PURPOSES ONLY.)
rulings.Rulings: (Part 2 of 2) (Tentative ruling continues from previous entry.) The moving party shall lodge with the clerk in Department 301 by the time set for this hearing a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling. Any party wishing to contest the tentative ruling must email contestasbestostr@sftc.org by 4:00 p.m. on the day before the hearing and state their intention to contest. If a hearing is requested, it will be on October 8, 2024, at 9:30 a.m. Attorneys may appear in person or remotely via zoom: Meeting ID 160 757 8308; Passcode: 485029. Face coverings are optional. The Court no longer provides a court reporter in the Law and Motion department. Parties may retain their own reporter, who may appear in the courtroom or remotely. A retained reporter must be a California certified court reporter (CSR), for only a CSR's transcript may be used in California courts. If a CSR is being retained, include in your email all of the following: Their name, CSR and telephone number, and their individual work email address. There will be only one official record. If the parties cannot agree, the Court will designate a qualified court reporter to provide the official transcript for the matter, and the party or parties will bear the cost. = (301/RCE)

rulings.Case Title: SIMMIE COLLINS VS. GENERAL ELECTRIC COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order Granting Preference In Setting Case For Trial, And Extending Discovery Cutoff
rulings.Rulings: On Asbestos Law and Motion Calendar for Tuesday, October 8, 2024, in Department 301, Line 2. Plaintiff's Motion for Order Granting Preference in Setting Case for Trial, and Extending Discovery Cutoff is GRANTED under C.C.P. section 36(a). 1. The trial date is January 27, 2025, at 11:15 a.m. in Department 206. a. Last day pursuant to C.C.P. section 36(f) is February 5, 2025. b. The parties shall follow the California Rules of Court, San Francisco Local Rules, and Local Rule 20. 2. The last day for hearing summary judgment/adjudication motions is January 21, 2025. a. Summary judgment/adjudication motions shall be brought on regular notice pursuant to the relevant provisions of the C.C.P., unless the parties stipulate otherwise. b. Before a party files and serves a summary judgment/adjudication motion, it must contact the clerk to make a reservation. c. The Court allows a maximum of four summary judgment/adjudication motions per day to be calendared, unless good cause is found to exceed this number. Contact the clerk to schedule a good cause hearing. 3. Time to respond to written discovery not yet served is shortened to 20 days. a. For written discovery that has already been served, responses are due within 20 days of this hearing or by the date determined by the C.C.P., whichever is earlier. b. Any issue/dispute that requires meet and confer, shall occur in person or via telephone, not by email or letter. 4. Electronic service is considered the equivalent of personal service. 5. The fact discovery cut-off date is January 10, 2025. 6. The expert discovery cut-off date is January 24, 2025. 7. All bankruptcy documents shall be turned over to defendants no later than November 8, 2024. a. If Plaintiff submits documents to bankruptcy trusts after this date, they must notify Defendants no later than five days after submission. b. The fact discovery cut-off does not apply to bankruptcy documents. 8. No later than October 22, 2024: a. Plaintiff must provide a statement to each Defendant stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of any facts supporting Plaintiff's claims as to each Defendant. b. Defendants must provide a statement stating the name and contact information of each lay person with knowledge of facts supporting Defendant's affirmative defenses. c. Any person not identified on the statements may be barred from testifying at trial or presenting a declaration on a summary judgment motion. (Tentative ruling continues in Part 2 of 2.)
